content.text: Sequential Tool Calls
Sequential tool calls occur when each call depends on the result of the previous one. The model cannot determine what to ask next until it sees what the prior tool returned — so it issues one
tool_use
block per turn, waits for the
tool_result
, reasons about that result, and only then decides the next step.

attrs.alt: Sequential tool call chain across multiple turns

content.text: Sequential calls require
N
round-trips for
N
tools — unavoidable when each step uses the prior result. The trade-off against parallel calls is not speed vs simplicity but
data dependency
: use sequential when step N+1 cannot run without step N's output.
Recognising Sequential Patterns in Responses
When you inspect a response from Claude, you can tell the model intends sequential execution because the response contains exactly one
tool_use
block (not multiple) and the natural-language text before it references information it hasn't retrieved yet:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

messages = [{"role": "user", "content": "Find the weather in NYC, suggest an outdoor activity, and book it for me."}]
tools = [
    {"name": "get_weather", "description": "Get current weather for a city",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["city"]}},
    {"name": "plan_activity", "description": "Suggest outdoor activities given weather",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"condition": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["condition"]}},
    {"name": "book_reservation", "description": "Book an activity",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"activity": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["activity"]}},
]

response = client.messages.create(model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=1024, tools=tools, messages=messages)

print(f"stop_reason: {response.stop_reason}")
print(f"tool_use blocks: {sum(1 for b in response.content if b.type == 'tool_use')}")
# stop_reason: tool_use
# tool_use blocks: 1   ← exactly one: sequential, not parallel
for block in response.content:
    if block.type == "tool_use":
        print(f"  calling: {block.name}({block.input})")
A single
tool_use
block is the model signalling: "I need this result before I can continue." Contrast with parallel calls where the response contains multiple
tool_use
blocks simultaneously.
Implementing a Sequential Agent Loop
The agent loop for sequential calls is the simplest possible: send messages, check
stop_reason
, execute the one tool, append the result, repeat until
end_turn
The loop naturally handles both sequential and parallel calls because it appends
all
tool_use
blocks from a response in one batch — when there are multiple blocks (parallel) they all go back together; when there is one block (sequential) only one result is sent.
Building the Tool Dispatcher
The
dispatch_tool
function used above maps tool names to implementations. For a sequential multi-step agent, each tool typically returns data that the next call depends on:
Tracking Call Depth

Long sequential chains consume many API round-trips. Add a depth counter and a maximum to prevent runaway loops — an agent that mistakenly chains indefinitely would burn tokens and time without bound:
In practice, well-designed tool sets with clear descriptions rarely exceed 4–6 turns for a user request. Values of 10–15 are a safe ceiling for most agents.
Inspecting the Full Conversation History
After a sequential run, the
messages
list captures every step. Printing it reveals how the model used prior results:
When Sequential Is the Right Choice
Sequential calls are required whenever the input to step N+1 depends on the output of step N. Common patterns:
Pattern
Why Sequential
Example
Look up → filter → act
Each step uses the prior result as input
Search products → pick cheapest → buy
Conditional branching
Next tool name depends on prior result
Check auth → if OK: read data; else: refresh token
Iterative refinement
Output of step N is input for N+1 of same tool
edit_file → lint → edit_file (repeat until clean)
Write-then-verify
Verification needs the written artifact ID
create_record → get_record(id) → validate_schema
Passing Context Between Sequential Calls
Because the full message history is included in each API call, every prior
tool_result

is visible to the model when it decides the next tool call. You don't need to manually extract data and inject it — the model reads the prior result directly from context. This simplifies the implementation but has two important consequences:
Result size matters
: A tool that returns a 50 KB JSON blob inflates every subsequent API call. Summarise or trim large results before returning them.
The model can cross-reference
: Step 3 can reference data from step 1 even if step 2 didn't use it — the model sees the full history, not just the immediately prior result.
If a tool's full output is required for the final answer but not for intermediate steps, consider storing it externally and returning only a reference ID — the model can call a
get_full_data(id)
tool at the end if the user needs the complete data.
Error Propagation in Sequential Chains
When a tool in the middle of a sequential chain fails, the model receives an
is_error: True

result. Whether the chain recovers depends on the task: sometimes the model retries with different parameters; sometimes it falls back to a different approach; sometimes it gives up and explains the failure to the user. All of these are valid — the agent loop doesn't need special logic for each case:

The model is quite good at recovering from tool failures mid-chain — it has the error message in context and can reason about corrective actions. If you observe the model spinning in a retry loop, add a
MAX_TOOL_TURNS
guard (as shown earlier) to break out after a reasonable number of attempts.
Testing Sequential Tool Chains

Unit-test each tool independently, then write an integration test that exercises the full chain with deterministic stub tools. Using stubs lets you verify the agent issues the right calls in the right order without making real API calls:

import pytest

# --- Stub tools for testing ---
call_log: list[str] = []

def stub_get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    call_log.append(f"get_weather({city})")
    return '{"city": "NYC", "temp_c": 22, "condition": "clear"}'

def stub_plan_activity(condition: str) -> str:
    call_log.append(f"plan_activity({condition})")
    return '{"activities": ["hiking"]}'

def stub_book_reservation(activity: str) -> str:
    call_log.append(f"book_reservation({activity})")
    return '{"confirmed": true, "id": "R9999"}'

STUB_REGISTRY = {
    "get_weather": lambda inp: stub_get_weather(**inp),
    "plan_activity": lambda inp: stub_plan_activity(**inp),
    "book_reservation": lambda inp: stub_book_reservation(**inp),
}

def test_sequential_order():
    """Verify the agent calls tools in dependency order."""
    call_log.clear()
    # Run agent with stub dispatcher
    result = run_agent_safe(
        "Weather in NYC then book something outdoor",
        tools=tools,  # same tool schemas, stubs handle execution
    )
    assert call_log[0].startswith("get_weather")
    assert call_log[1].startswith("plan_activity")
    assert call_log[2].startswith("book_reservation")
    assert "R9999" in result
Stub-based tests run in milliseconds and catch regressions in call order, argument passing, and result propagation. Run them in CI alongside unit tests for each tool implementation.
Debugging with Step-by-Step Logging

When a sequential chain produces an unexpected final answer, the root cause is almost always in an intermediate result. Add structured logging to capture each tool call and result at the time it happens rather than reconstructing it from the final messages list:
With this logger, each tool call emits a structured JSON line containing the tool name, input, result length, and duration. Piping those logs into a structured log viewer (e.g.
jq
or Datadog) makes it easy to spot which step produced an unexpected result — without re-running the full chain.
Performance Considerations
Sequential chains incur one API round-trip per tool call. For a 3-step chain with 300ms model latency and 200ms tool latency per step, total wall-clock time is:
If any of the 3 steps could safely run in parallel, switching them to a parallel call reduces the round-trips:
For homogeneous tool latencies this is
— a 3x speedup. The takeaway:
default to sequential
(simpler, correct for dependent tasks), then identify which steps are truly independent and move those to a parallel call.
Summary
Sequential tool calls are indicated by exactly one
tool_use
block in a response — the model needs the result before continuing.
Each result is fed back as a
tool_result
user message, and the model reasons about it before deciding the next call.
The agent loop is the same for sequential and parallel calls — just append all
tool_result
blocks in a single user message and repeat.
Always add a
MAX_TOOL_TURNS
guard to prevent infinite loops caused by buggy tool implementations or ambiguous tasks.
Use sequential calls whenever the next step's inputs cannot be determined without the prior step's output — for purely independent tasks, prefer parallel calls to save round-trips.
